Impact on Oil Market: Iraq invades Kuwait, freezing exports from both nations and causing a sharp, brief price spike.
OPEC raises output quotas just as the Asian Financial Crisis hits, triggering a severe global price crash.
Attacks cause immediate aviation fuel demand destruction, followed by long-term geopolitical risk premiums in the Middle East.
US-led invasion severely disrupts Iraqi oil infrastructure, taking millions of barrels offline and tightening global supply.
Crude prices peak at an all-time high of $147 per barrel before plunging below $40 due to global demand destruction.
Regional uprisings disrupt Libyan sweet crude exports, pushing global oil prices back above $100 per barrel.
OPEC refuses to cut production, launching a price war against US shale that crashes global crude prices.
OPEC partners with 10 non-OPEC nations, including Russia, to form OPEC+ and implement coordinated output cuts.
Drone strikes on Saudi Aramco facilities temporarily knock out 5% of global oil supply, causing a record intraday price spike.
Russia refuses proposed output cuts, prompting Saudi Arabia to launch a brief price war and flood the market.
Pandemic lockdowns destroy global fuel demand, causing WTI crude futures to crash into negative territory (-$37.63) for the first time.
Russia invades Ukraine, triggering Western sanctions, self-sanctioning by buyers, and major structural re-routing of global crude flows.
Outbreak of war in Gaza injects a persistent geopolitical risk premium into oil markets due to fears of wider Middle East escalation.
The United Arab Emirates formally leaves the alliance to maximize production capacity utilization, realigning Gulf market shares.
Remaining alliance members recalibrate voluntary output cuts to balance shifting compliance levels and non-OPEC supply surges.
